design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect dropped items while avoiding enemies; eat spinach to temporarily become invincible and attack the primary antagonist
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure on item collection combined with persistent enemy pursuit; resource scarcity of single spinach can per level gates offensive capability
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Play as Popeye the Sailor, using puzzle-solving platforming navigation and strategic spinach timing to overcome Brutus and environmental hazards
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately removes jump button unlike contemporary platformers (Donkey Kong), forcing players to use stairs/ladders exclusively; invincibility power-up is single-use gate to victory rather than general advantage; no twitch reflexes required, emphasizes spatial puzzle-solving and enemy pattern recognition

difficulty.lives_system: Player loses a life when hit by Brutus, flying objects (bottles, vultures, skulls), or fails to collect dropped item within time limit after it reaches bottom of screen
difficulty.checkpointing: Three distinct screens per cycle with progressive difficulty; each cycle increases challenge through item collection requirements and enemy behavior
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: No difficulty selection; spinach timing and enemy avoidance patterns are core skill gates; punching bag mechanic on first screen of each cycle offers optional score optimization

progression.upgrade_system: Spinach power-up per level grants temporary invincibility and doubles point value of items; no persistent upgrades
progression.unlock_mechanism: Three screens per cycle; progression requires collecting all items on screen before advancing; cycle repeats with increased item count requirements (24 hearts → 16 musical notes → 24 letters spelling HELP)
replayability_hooks: Arcade-style score chasing on each level
Optional punching bag mechanic for bonus points
Three distinct screen layouts with different item configurations

world_building_techniques: Licensed IP (Popeye comic strip characters) provides implicit narrative context
Visual character presence (Olive Oyl dropping items, Brutus chasing, Sea Hag superimposed)
Level themes reflect character dynamics (Olive as ally, Brutus as antagonist, Swee'Pea and Wimpy as background elements)

boss_design.philosophy: Primary antagonist Brutus serves as persistent threat rather than discrete boss encounter; Sea Hag appears as environmental hazard on later levels; spinach power-up is mandatory gate to deal meaningful damage
notable_bosses: Brutus - primary pursuing antagonist with climbing and vertical jump attack capabilities; stunnable via punching bag mechanic
Sea Hag - stationary environmental hazard on later levels (noted as superimposed rather than integrated, per unused graphics showing original skull-stack design)

faction_indicators: Neutral/Background: Wimpy, Swee'Pea (licensed characters without mechanical role)
danger_signals: Flying hazards (bottles, vultures, skulls) telegraphed from top of screen
environmental_storytelling: Three visually distinct screens per cycle establish location variety
Punching bag/bucket mechanic implies environmental interactivity beyond core traversal
Spinach as singular power-up reinforces Popeye IP core fantasy

sources_used: wikipedia - Popeye (video game) - Gameplay, Development, Legacy sections
tcrf - Popeye (NES) - Unused Graphics (Sea Hag skull stack reference)
